flashcards.back: Big-O (O) gives an upper bound on growth: f(n)=O(g(n)) iff ∃c>0,n0 such that 0≤f(n)≤c·g(n) for all n≥n0. It is used to describe the worst-case time (or space) of an algorithm.
Big-Omega (Ω) provides a lower bound: f(n)=Ω(g(n)) iff ∃c>0,n0 such that 0≤c·g(n)≤f(n) for all n≥n0. It captures the best-case or minimum growth rate of an algorithm.
Big-Theta (Θ) denotes a tight bound: f(n)=Θ(g(n)) iff f(n)=O(g(n)) and f(n)=Ω(g(n)). It means the algorithm’s growth is exactly of order g(n).
Little-o (o) indicates a strictly smaller order: f(n)=o(g(n)) iff ∀c>0,∃n0 such that 0≤f(n)<c·g(n) for n≥n0. In other words, f grows strictly slower than g.
Little-omega (ω) denotes a strictly larger order: f(n)=ω(g(n)) iff ∀c>0,∃n0 such that 0≤c·g(n)<f(n) for n≥n0. Thus f grows strictly faster than g.

flashcards.front: List the common asymptotic complexity classes from fastest to slowest.
flashcards.back: O(1) constant, O(log n) logarithmic, O(n) linear, O(n log n) linearithmic, O(n^2) quadratic, O(n^3) cubic, O(2^n) exponential, O(n!) factorial. These cover typical algorithm runtimes.

flashcards.front: State the key properties of Big-O, Big-Ω and Big-Θ.
flashcards.back: O and Ω are reflexive (f=O(f)) and transitive (if f=O(g) and g=O(h) then f=O(h)). Θ is reflexive, transitive and symmetric (f=Θ(g) ⇔ g=Θ(f)). Moreover, f=O(g) ∧ g=O(f) ⇔ f=Θ(g).

flashcards.front: Define space complexity and give an example of its relation to time complexity.
flashcards.back: Space complexity measures memory usage as a function of input size, including auxiliary space plus input. For example, merge sort runs in O(n log n) time but needs O(n) extra space, while quicksort uses O(log n) stack space on average, showing the time-space trade-off.
